pattern: The process is fully deterministic -- always merge the leftmost minimum-sum adjacent pair -- so the answer is just the count of merges a direct simulation performs before the array happens to become non-decreasing.
hints: At each step you have no real choice: scan the current array for the adjacent pair with the smallest sum, breaking ties by taking the leftmost one, and replace those two entries with their sum.
After every merge, check whether the (now shorter) array is non-decreasing; if it is, stop and report how many merges you performed.

Because the array shrinks by one element each merge and the allowed length is tiny, a direct rebuild-and-rescan simulation is fast enough -- the challenge is just getting the leftmost-minimum-sum tie-break exactly right.

edge_inputs.description: a single element is trivially sorted
several adjacent pairs tie for the minimum sum, so the leftmost one must be chosen first
strictly decreasing values, forcing repeated merges from the right before the array can settle
negative numbers whose pair sums are more negative than any positive pairing, so merging starts from the negative cluster
a longer mixed sequence needing several rounds of merging

stress.description: random arrays across the full allowed length and value range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(50, size))
    nums = [rng.randint(-1000, 1000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]
